commentBody: I first thought 58D was clued incorrectly but TIL even though there are 7 emirates in the UAE, there are only 6 ruling families and Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah are both ruled by branches of the same dynasty/family.
Whoopie! It wasn’t tough, but it was fun nonetheless. A lot of twists and turns and it was fun figuring out the spanners. Thank you.
Very funny, John and Jeff.  LESS is more, I guess. No MISTAKE: a BEAUTY, but not much of a STRUGGLE this SEASON.

Usually clues asking for the first name of a current member of congress is a gimme, but for some reason, I thought it was Jeremy Raskin, which was too many letters. Pretty easy puzzle otherwise, though I've never heard of a SKUA

It was very easy to solve this one without understanding the theme because there was little difficulty generating sufficient crosses to simply fill in the themed entries.  I prefer puzzles like last Sunday’s where figuring out the theme makes a difference in how easy the puzzle is to solve.
I really liked it.
Fun theme :)

OK, folks, SAYIT!

Did that itty-bitty three letter coffee klatch make a MUG outta you?

Like me, did'ja make  a CARELESSMISTAKE?

CUP?**
KEG?
URN?
POT?
JAR?
CAN?
POD?
TIN?
BAG?

**guess a Charger ain't a CUSTOMCAR, unless you paint it pink and call it Mary Kay 😉

Nice variation on a Thursday: figuring out the clues rather than the answers, but it made for an easier puzzle. TAZO was the word that took me the longest to remember, as I haven't frequented a Starbucks in decades.

Thanks, John and Jeff!
Never got thr gimmick. Even so I found this to be a pretty easy puzzle.

I solved this puzzle in just over 12 minutes -- by far a personal Thursday record -- and it wasn't a whole lot of fun. The mildly interesting theme wasn't worth the pedestrian fill. Elks? Will there be mooses next week? I'm not really grumpy, I'm just disappointed.

This could have been a Tuesday.  Definitely not worthy of a Thursday.  Can we PLEASE get a rebus puzzle or something similar for Thursdays again?

This ‘theme’ was so weak that I barely noticed that it was there.
I wonder how EEL is used as a metaphor for slippery.
Now *that’s* some clever word play. Impressive!
Did the puzzle with just the fill; even at the end barely understood the theme, which makes it less fun.

My CARELESS MISTAKEs:

1-Thinking of The Metamorphosis by Kafka (which obviously didn't fit) instead of  Metamorphoses by OVID (which I wasn't familiar with.)

2-Reading [____bien] in French instead of Spanish and going for "très" before "Être" before the aha of ESTÁ when the crosses weren't working.

Aside from the two mistakes, all went swimmingly (even MUG instead of urn by some stroke of luck) and devoid of  ENDLESS STRUGGLEs.

I would have preferred a tad more bite in the cluing of a Thursday puzzle, but I'm a very satisfied solver just the same because I loved the idea for the theme and the high of figuring it out. 

J&J together are twice the powerhouse that they are when they construct on their own.  This puzzle, even without the added crunch, is proof of that. 

Thank you, gents. 
You're always brilliant!
They're really pulling out all the cliche fill today huh.

The standard of slipperiness ought to be the hagfish. https://www.nhm.ac.uk/discover/how-much-slime-can-a-hagfish-make.html Either that, or my granddaughter when she finds a piece of candy and doesn't want you to see her with it.
Cute theme, but solved this in record time for a Thursday.
A new personal best.  I think it has been a few years since I've had one of those for any day of the week.
I truly love a puzzle where I finish it without understanding the theme at all.

Finished fast but had to read the column to understand the theme. I couldn't figure out what a "bad carer move" was (I missed an E) or what a "winless season" is (sports, apparently). On the other hand, I'm always happy to be reminded of elk bugling, and MUG got me just as I was grumbling to myself "who even calls it a coffee urn, anyway."
Did I really just solve a Jeff Chen Thursday puzzle in Wednesday time? Apparently so, but it's just hard to believe. It took me a while to get the theme, but even that wasn't too hard, in the end.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
14 minutes of meh.

Minimally contributory theme to the solve (which is common enough but never joyful) save for a suspicion of recurring LESSes which hit me halfway through but were not compelling enough to bother streamlining the solve with... and, worse, internally inconsistent theme fill.

BAD CAREER MOVE is certainly and always a MISTAKE, say.
WINTER is unarguably and ever a SEASON.
PAGEANT QUEEN is by definition always a BEAUTY (for crossword purposes).

But ENDEAVOR is hardly tautologous with STRUGGLE.

As a square is always a rectangle but a rectangle is only sometimes a square, a STRUGGLE is always an ENDEAVOR, but low-stress, artistic, worthy and simple ENDEAVORS are hardly STRUGGLES.

This was not a frabjous Thursday for this solver.

There is zero chance these two hall of fame constructors had a hand in the cluing. I know many yesterday felt that it was Monday level clueing.  Today’s was even easier. 

If I’m being 100. The theme was pretty underwhelming (again, given the constructor names).  

I literally started to slow down because I was afraid I was going to set a personal best. And I didn’t want it to be on this puzzle.  Fortunately I slowed down enough. 

I know this post sounds so arrogant to some. But even xwstats said this was easy. The hardest part of today’s puzzle was trying to figure out how to spell SINISE. 

Yikes bro. Just yikes.
No idea what that was all about, but done is done, so I'm outta here.
I got it. I had to read the constructor’s notes to understand it. Kind of a stretch, but a satisfying one.

OK. Well, I solved it. On a whim I checked stats. Unbeknownst to me my streak of many years was apparently broken 3 weeks ago although I have not missed a day or a solve. My addiction may finally be over! Yes I know I can appeal to the staff, explain the issue, etc. I love the NYT, the puzzles, constructors, commenters, and columnists, but one shouldn’t have to work this hard to play and make sure the stats are right. Suddenly I feel so free!
Usually there is an "aha" moment where I see the clever theme of the puzzle, but not this time. Even after solving it, I didn't really "get" it until reading the explanation.
Well I liked this one!  Fun and just tricky enough for me.
Is the bien clue French or Spanish est à or esta. Est à bien is a bit odd but didn't think bien was a Spanish word

Thursday PB 12:25 🙌

Glacial for some I’m sure.
Workout?  Thursday is the new Tuesday. Gives me time to clean the verdigris from my pre-1982 pennies.

I thought my woodworking hobbyist husband had every tool on the planet, but today I learned a new one. And I may be the only only person who has never stepped foot inside a Starbucks, so that crossing was no help. That was my only real hiccup in what I’d call a semi-challenging puzzle.

But I thought the regular fill was supposed to help with solving the theme, not the other way around? For me, the theme clues were the easiest part, and those were some of my earliest entries. It was a decent puzzle, just a little disappointing as a Thursday.
When I finally got the theme, I let out an audible AHA. Satisfying theme indeed!

I liked the concept of the theme but in terms of a Thursday puzzle this was a big disappointment.  With the repeating LESS string, the simple "trick" and the Monday/Tuesday level cluing I can't see any reason to run this on a Thursday.  

Oh, NYT crossword, what have you become?
I was nearly undone by misreading “garish” instead of garnish, which mean two very different things in terms of amount.
Hard Tuesday, fine Wednesday, weak Thursday...

I'd be interested to see if anyone else feels the crosswords are beginning to feel dumbed-down? I used to be a 4/7 each week, now I'm hard-pressed not to be able to finish one. Thursdays, Fridays, and Saturdays in particular have been excessively weak lately. Is there a large turnover of puzzle creators? Are puzzles published on merit, or some 'other' metric nowadays? The NYT crossword used to be at the pinnacle. The best way I can describe it recently is 'meh...'.
Enjoyed the poetic and soothing feel to the sounds of the theme answers. Nice to have a stressLESS Thursday that still contained some aha moments.
Hmm. Even after reading the explanation, I'm a little unsteady re: the theme, but I solved in near record time, so I guess I'm still pretty smart...??

I saw lots of SKUAS on holiday in Shetland, where they are called bonxies. They are very aggressive sea birds who will dive bomb you if you go too near their nests.

I enjoyed this one, though I had to look up brands and names. It was very much at my level, which means rather easy for some here. I liked the POET clue and I learnt that my 'tut, tut' is 'tsk, tsk' but I think you make the same sort of sound - a dental click.

When our boys were little
we rarely left them and 
I felt so very guilty when we did.

Hubby booked a weekend getaway
and the boys stayed with my mom.

When we returned,
I expected my poor, neglected babes
to rush to the arms of their long-lost mamma.

Instead, we found them in 
new, handmade aprons,
rolling dough to make cookies
with ARLO Guthrie children’s songs playing,
and sipping fruit smoothies.

She’d even taken the basement door off
and laid it on crates so they had a “table”
that was *just* the right height.


CC’s wildly over-inflated sense of importance?
Beautifully checked.

Nitpick: I wouldn't call Lovely Rita a "hit." It may be a well-loved song by many, but it's a track on the Sergeant Pepper album that was never released as a single. In my experience, in that era, for a mere album track to be considered a "hit," it has to REALLY stand out -- e.g., tons of radio airplay (Led Zeppelin's "Stairway to Heaven," for example).

I haven't read the Wordplay column yet. 

I finished this puzzle very quickly, but I still have no idea how the trick works. I'm going to go back and try to figure it out. I'm assuming it involves "something less something," coming up with two other words suggested by the two "somethings," then removing the second one from the first???

I don't think this has ever happened to me before. I've been unable to solve the Thursday trick a few times, but that always prevented me from completing the puzzle.

This was nearly an ENDLESS STRUGGLE for me. I got tripped up in the SW corner. Had ADZE and TAZO, but just couldn’t come up with the rest until SPEW finally came to me. This one was just right for me, though I’m sorry it’s frustrating so many of you.
I'm a newbie and I finished this one with the help of a few little lookups--like of proper names. And I finished it despite thinking--at first--that 6D [Chargers and Challengers] were sports teams.

Guess the supply of Thursday-esque puzzles is depleted again. A very solid Wednesday puzzle, with a clever theme.

There were six Ks in the grid.

1A [OBT____]

USELESSINFORMATION

Two incredibly experienced puzzle constructors produce a decent Wednesday-level puzzle.
Something is not quite right in Crosslandia.

Ah, Thursday puzzles!  How I once looked forward to you all week.
Where have you gone?  When will you come back?
I miss you.

I'd hate to say that this puzzle was a SORRY SIGHT, but it was a bit of a disappointment.

I got the theme after completing the second theme answer. Completely underwhelming puzzle. I expected more when I saw the names of the constructors. Lots of tired fill, such as KIR, EVEL, ARLO, ECO. I learned SKUA and ADZE from the puzzles many years ago. 

As for JAMIE, I was surprised that several commenters were unaware of him. Maybe I've become too much of a political junkie since 45 and 47, but Jamie Raskin is in the news a lot.
Quick and easy one to get the brain juices flowing this AM

Well... not all that easy for me, but a good Thursday workout. I was appropriately puzzled about the theme for quite a while but finally caught on. No big deal. One quite unusual puzzle find today - a Wednesday from February 22, 2017 by Kyle Dolan. Some theme clue and answer examples: "...SLID SAID SAND SANE SINE NINE ..." SLIMTONONE "...DOTE DOLE DOLL DELL SELL..." NOTETOSELF "...HERD HEAD HEAT SEAT STAT..." HERETOSTAY "...OMEN OPEN OPED SPED SHED SHAD SHAM WHAM WHAT..." AMENTOTHAT Here's that link... https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/22/2017&g=36&d=D I'm done. ....

This is really getting old.

This was a puzzle with a Wednesday-level theme and Monday-level cluing. I know for a fact that both these constructors can do better, so this is absolutely an editing issue.

For inexperienced solvers who like an easier puzzle, look at it this way: The Times is basically saying you will never be good enough to solve a real Thursday puzzle, let alone a Saturday puzzle. They are saying you will never improve, so you should not even try. They throw you the easiest possible puzzles, because they think you are not intelligent enough to handle the tough ones. They are insulting you to your face. Are you going to stand for it?

Got the theme with 27A. Went along well, except for I misspelt SINeSE (sorry Gary), and that had me wondering whether 19D was sage, safe, or SANE, and also what in the world we were going for with the Christmas story. Got it sorted.
I don't complain about difficulty levels, lest one come along and leave me in tears!
Have a great day!

Well, bLESSed be.  A LESSon to be learned? LESS is more. Or maybe we are children of a LESSer god.

LESS-than-Thursday difficulty anyway.
Which is okay for me today, I have a headache. The wind has shifted and we are once again getting strong smoke from the forest fires just west of us.

I don't want to pile on with 'the puzzles are getting easier' gang, but... the puzzles are getting easier. I am a long-time solver, and by that I mean it takes me a long time to solve Th, Fri, Sat puzzles. Over the years, I have enjoyed seeing my average solve times go down. But lately it hasn't been as much fun as part of the joy was being challenged and meeting that challenge. It almost feels like cheating these days.

Clever theme as I would expect from these solvers and a solid grid with some nice accessory fill. Nothing too tricky on the clueing, although for some reason I was spacing out on the NW. I enjoyed it.

I enjoyed the theme today, good "Aha" moment when it all clicks.

Some of the filler clues I thought were a bit off.  I struggled a bit in the SW corner, because I'm not sure how LAY LOW makes sense for its clue or SNAP.  And SAY IT made sense but it felt imprecise.

Also in NW since I don't know anything about euchre I thought maybe it might have something to do with TAROT.  Then when I had MAR____ONE I of course thought MARSCAPONE before MARROW BONE.

Good puzzle overall, I just thought that some of the clues that were crossed with the proper noun and trivia clues were a bit imprecise making it hard to get those crossings.
I confidently filled in URN as well!  The crosses soon corrected me.
Thank you, Jeff and John!  Great puzzle; I enjoyed the tricks.

The plural of elk is elk. 
That is all.

I didn't find this as easy as many of the commenters. Had no idea about SKUA, didn't tumble to SNAP and SPEW for quite a while, and somehow mentally misread BAD ___ER MOVE so much that despite figuring out the gimmick with ENDEAVOR, and ultimately solving the puzzle, I was *still* misreading it as CARE-ER and could make neither heads nor tails of it for what felt like ages.
I gave not a thought to what was going on with the themers and so a zippy solve with the fun at the end figuring out what exactly was going on.   Nicely done and thanks.

Per the Midi:

A "know-it-all" is just someone who has memorized a bunch of stuff. You can memorize baseball statistics and trivia going back a hundred years and not be a "genius" (although there's a good chance you're somewhere on the spectrum).

A genius more of a "figure-out-more-than-anyone-else-has-given-the-same-information" kind of person.

Close enough for a crossword, though.
I found elks annoying, since the normal plural is elk.

The Downs were a blessing as I WINkled out the long themers... and wasn't THAT a dainty dish to set before...well, all of us!!?
When I finished and looked at the Constructors' names, I did kind of go, "Oh, no wonder!"  But still....I think this represents a New High.  I don't recall anything like it in my decades of solving; in fact *this is worthy of the Charles M. Deber Prize for Unique Crossword Puzzles!*  (Okay, I invented the prize, but Dr. Deber was one-of-a-kind--every puzzle unique, mind-bending, and admirable.
Wow, John and Jeff.  Thank you!

And thanks for those who were patient with my little rant yesterday; (pfft  to "Relax!")  I cannot do anything about the economy, the Supreme Court conservatives, the gerrymandering, the dismantling of The Constitution, or the weather that razed places in MS last night...but I can correct what amounts to disrespect for a lovely tradition--the LEI.  It is a decorative gesture of welcome, of farewell, of appreciation, and it is made of flowers (sometimes with leaves)  that have two qualities:  fragrance and a degree of staying power.  
There are other (accurate) ways to clue HIBISCUS, which lacks those characteristics, despite being ubiquitous.  (We had a 50-ft hedge of red hibiscus beside our quarters at Schofield Barracks on Oahu.)

Got me thinking about my favorite Green films on coppers.

5. Serpico - A cop saves water by not shaving and uncovers the corruption of his fellow officers who throw dirty coffee cups/mugs/urns into the recycling bin.

4. The French Connection - Gene Hackman and Roy Scheider spend two hours chasing a car with a broken muffler.

3. The Departed - Matt Damon and Leonardo DiCaprio, two great-looking actors known for their environmentalism as well as their timely delivery play coppers doing copper stuff.

2. RoboCop - Detroit's new environmental policies lead to the police department to resurrect, rebuild and reuse dead officers.

1. Fargo - Fascinating documentary about creating mulch and compost with a wood chipper.

0. Speed - A copper struggles with his conscience when he must drive a bus faster than is ecologically beneficial.

Curse you, ADSE/TASO for wasting about 2 minutes of my time spotting you!

Also, I was also pretty confident in URN for too long. I got MUGged.
Another happy Thursday solve!
21 across should be JVC?? Phillips' big moment was in the 80s.

I guess that the theme today was just too clever for me. I solved the puzzle, but had to come here to find out what was going on with the theme, and even having all the pieces I couldn’t get it but had to read the explanation for each one.
Yes, I read the column, but I still don't see how "Winter" is a clue for WINLESS SEASON. You can have a winless season any time of the year (depending on when your sport is played).
Enjoyed the theme. But the rest of the puzzle was a little meh.
I figured out the theme, but couldn't complete the puzzle without some slight assistance. One day!!

Poem made from words in the clues and answers of today's puzzle

a/ who can speak to the story of the mountain
a pageant of dinosaurs?
the pioneering ladybug?
the earthquake?
d/ in the journey of the seabird
the seabird carried the families of the nation
and the breeze carried the seabird
and something spoken carried the breeze
that something that the
a/ poet lost

Good puzzle, neat theme. For all you who come here to comment on how the puzzles are getting easier - have you considered that maybe they're not, and you're just getting better at solving them? I don't know if it's true, but it's what I'm gonna go with.

Ok like 37A I admit defeat 😞. Finished almost 7 minutes faster than my average but doesn’t reflect the end less struggle 🤷‍♀️. I kinda got the theme but it just didn’t click. However as always I so admire the work and genius that goes into the construction of a puzzle 👏🎉👏. 
But TIL one of my favorite teas was once owned by Starbucks 🫖!

SEN____NTAL JOURNEY
TIMELESS CLASSIC

I know, too easy, but it was the best I could come up with.
Complete JUST over the 10 min mark, took a look around, and WHOOSH!
The plural of Elk is Elk, not Elks.

I didn't get the gag until well after finishing the puzzle. I didn't like the gag. I didn't want the gag. I am not a fan of the gag. It's -- what's going on here? Some letters are missing? Some sort of all caps type situation? Some mysterious evasiveness, such that I'm supposed to guess, based only on "EAVOR" that I'm supposed to write "ENDLESSSTRUGGLE." Yeah, right. The odds of anyone guessing that without at least 9 down crosses are low. 
I have previously in this column had occasion to note that I don't like this kind of puzzle, the sort that in my view prioritizes a laborious gimmick over the commonplace and no doubt plebian approach of **just making a bunch of words that cross and clues about those words.**
When I have so said, I have been met with explanations as to why I'm in the wrong, explanations of the puzzle, accusations of being an enemy of fun, and one or two up-thumbses. 
I can't wait to find out what today brings. Hit it.

I loved the puzzle and the theme! I love it when themes ACTUALLY help you solve the puzzle especially when they also make sense… 

For me, this puzzle had enough challenges that without knowing that the theme entries all had LESS in them would have been much more difficult.

Well done!

Rating:
9/10
The plural of elk is. ELK.
It’s a great solving day when my two favorite muscle cars make an appearance.
Fun puzzle.  Just enough to get there.  Clever!

May I? 

The clue, once you have filled in the blanks, refers to the second part of the answer. It does not refer to the whole answer. The second part of the answer follows LESS.

27A. The clue is WINTER, after you have filled in the blanks. The answer is SEASON. 

The clue WINTER is NOT referring to WINLESS SEASON, only SEASON.

The letters WIN that come before LESS are what are missing in the clues, you insert WIN in the blanks.

Yes, the answers taken as a whole are common expressions, but the meaning of the expression is irrelevant to understanding the relationship of the clue, once you fill in the blanks, to the answer, which, again, is only the second part of the answer, after LESS.

I hope this helps!
TIL that I've been spelling my US representative's first name incorrectly. Thank you, osso bucco. I'm sooo embarrassed.

I did it! Broke my 365 day streak by intentionally skipping Tuesday and Wednesday. Now my streak is just “54 Thursdays in a row.” Yay! Was afraid I might not be able to keep my promise to myself. Fun Thursday. No need to get the theme to guess the theme entries, but clever.
The plural of "elk" is elk.

And yet another late puzzle find. A Tuesday from May 22, 2007 by Roger Wolff. One down answer (straightforwardly clued) was: EDELWEISS And then three 15 letter across answers, clued as... Fun puzzle!

“Somewhere in my cobwebbed brain, a lonely neuron fired.” 

really made me laugh. I relate to this extremely.

SKUAS are vicious and territorial birds! This clue reminded me of a walking vacation in the very remote Shetland Isles, far north of the Scottish mainland, where my wife and I were dive-bombed by skuas protecting their nest. In escaping them I fell into a peat bog and emerged with my pants soaked. I took the risk of completing our hike in just my underwear, since we’d not seen another soul. My wife is still traumatized on lots of levels!

Has anyone yet pointed out (erroneously, but that's a topic for another conversation) that the one and only plural for ELK is ELK? Because that is the one and only plural they have ever heard of, and if THEY haven't heard of ELKS, it must not exist? 

Asking for my BPOE friends.

@HCTabak I still don’t understand the theme. Is the theme “take the first word of each theme answer, subtract the letters ‘less’, and then plug the resulting word into the blank in each theme clue”? Or is there some more elegant part of the theme that I’m missing?
@Bryan no, the letters before “LESS” are the letters that are missing from the clues. The answer “winless season” completes the clue___TER. does that help?

As Jake says, you take the "win" from WINLESS SEASON and put it into ___TER, and you get the name of a season.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Nancy J.

I was angry at the way the Times patronizes us.

And wrote the first anti-AP adjective I could think of.

Uncool.

@Nancy J.

And he was ridiculing the level of cluing, not any children.
@dutchiris summed my feeling up perfectly, although the clue for POET made it worth my time

@M. Biggen

Right? My streak was much shorter but abandoning it felt great. Not having to flyspeck Sundays, feeling free to check and reveal entries, skipping puzzles I'm not enjoying - it's awsome. The streak is a joyless trap.

@Andrzej I knew I liked you. Thanks for the affirmation. Trying to solve the puzzle of why my streak disappeared is futile and truly a wasted effort in a world that has more pressing issues. It would be like calling the phone company for ANY reason. 

I feel quite sad at losing the streak. I was ridiculously (though secretly) proud of it. But at the same time I feel like now maybe I could read a book at night, paint the bedroom, talk to my spouse, or learn Polish. Oh, the humanity!

@M. Biggen

I had an absurd Wordle streak until 2 years ago which was broken by a time zone glitch when I flew back to the UK from a visit to the US West Coast.

It is liberating indeed. I’ve only played occasionally since when my daughter wants to have a go at the puzzle together. 

The whole solve time and streak thing is a bit silly when you think about it. I did crosswords the old fashioned way for years. I was just as satisfied when I finished them, in an unknown time, occasionally with errors I'm sure. All too often my satisfaction now depends on a desperate hunt for a keying error as the clock ticks up.

@Andrzej

Agreed. I tend to try and puzzle every day because I like a gold star when I have done a puzzle without any help. 

If I use google I ‘spoil’ the puzzle with a lookup so I only get a blue one. And yes, if get bored the puzzle is left unfinished. So my streak is meaningless. 

I still can’t resist hunting down keying errors to mark it done in some way though.

@Tony

I no longer do Wordle, but when I still did, I used to break my streak on purpose every few days to not let it terrorize me.

On the crossword's page, upper right,  is a little cog that is widely used to show 'Settings'. Click on that and you will be offered, supreme among all lures, the ability to remove the timer from the page's display. Just pressing it lowers your heart rate immediately.

@John
Good point.  it could easily have been clued as a just a "song" .

@John

The "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band" album was an enormous hit so I think that any song on that album could be called a hit too. I know that I listened to the album and the song lots. Do you know that impromptu kazoos made of tissue paper and combs were used in the recording?

Here's what I found on Wikipedia about it:
[... it spent 27 weeks at number one on the Record Retailer chart in the United Kingdom and 15 weeks at number one on the Billboard Top LPs chart in the United States. In 1968, it won four Grammy Awards, including Album of the Year, the first rock LP to receive this honour; in 2003, it was inducted into the National Recording Registry by the Library of Congress for being "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant". It has topped several critics' and listeners' polls for the best album of all time, including those published by Rolling Stone magazine and in the book All Time Top 1000 Albums, and the UK's "Music of the Millennium" poll. More than 32 million copies had been sold worldwide as of 2011. It remains one of the best-selling albums of all time and was, as of 2018, the UK's best-selling studio album. A remixed and expanded edition of the album was released in 2017.]

@Bruce 

I figured it out. I'd really overthought it.

@Cherry
I was so pleased to finish a Thursday puzzle without cheats, but all those complaining that this puzzle is too easy would rob me of my pleased pride, if I let them.  I found this a pleasingly sufficient challenge.

@Bruce
When I worked the Olympics, I was the guy who had to tell the sun-burned masses why the bus to the satellite lot was late, because I had the walkie-talkie and the megaphone. Accident on 285, at least once a day. I blame out-of-towners, because the local drivers were usually quite courteous.

@Bruce
If you were on 285 or 85 during lockdown, the high speed drag racing was out of control. The gangs used souped-up black SUVs to clear lanes while the super speeders took over. It was best to just stay your lane while they zipped around you. Motorcycles did the same.

@lucky13

@Jerry

Although I did NYT puzzles years ago with a pencil on the subway, maybe, I've only been doing the puzzles again for about ten or eleven months, I guess. Is that a newbie?

I'll consider myself promoted when I can do more puzzles without ANY lookups. It seems to me that lately I can get though more of them--with a little bit of help. But I think I've only finished Mondays and Tuesdays without any lookups.

Or am I not understanding your question?

@lucky13

Oh, and this past Sunday, too.

And speaking of the Beatles (Lovely Rita), here's another one of their tunes: 

You've got to admit it's getting better
A little better all the time
(It can't get no worse)

@The X-Phile

For me it evokes a typical pitching performance in an era in which starters barely last six innings.  ;-)

@lucky13

This isn't in line with the theme. If I restore USE to your clue, I get OBTUSE, which doesn't connect to INFORMATION at all.

To better appreciate today's theme, consider, for example, 
P___ANT QUEEN and AGELESSBEAUTY. If you restore AGE to the clue you get PAGEANT QUEEN, and a pageant queen is typically quite the beauty. So a P___ANT QUEEN is an "age"-less beauty.

Based on the comments, it seems a lot of folks didn't really understand the theme, or appreciate its cleverness.

@Xword Junkie agree with your (constructive) criticism of the comment above. Also, in what world is it a valid critique to say that a word or name in the NYT Xword is “useless information”?!? First, the premise of insisting on “useful information” is bananas (ie why would you go, of all places in this information-rich world, to the *crossword* to find “useful info”?) and second, why is the name of an elected federal officeholder “useless”? Utility is both subjective AND not the point of DOING A PUZZLE. People need to just learn the new info and move on with their day, or stop seeking a specific type of utility from a short daily diversion.

@Xword Junkie
What version of the puzzle are you looking at, and where did you find it?
If you just wanted to insult the Maryland member of Congress, surely there are easier ways.  
And then there's the card game....

@Xword Junkie

I'm saying that USELESSINFORMATION is OBTUSE. I don't understand your problem with that. Just like an AGELESSBEAUTY can be a PAGENTQUEEN, some information can be OBTUSE.

Let me think of an example: when I listen to the weather report, they sometimes tell me what the weather was on this day in 1927, say. For me, that's USELESS, just not relevant to anything that matters to me. 

By the way, my statement had nothing to do with any of the other answers in today's puzzle.

I was just trying to be charming because we had OBTUSER in the puzzle the other day.

One man's meat....

@Laura Whitaker Why would you say that I said that a word or name in the puzzle is USELESS? I never said that and I certainly would never say that an elected official's name is useless. I was just talking in general. Let me give an example: my mother would say to me what "they" are wearing or what "they" are doing. I would say: "Who is [they]"? It's OBTUSE. Actually, I constantly find utility from the puzzles. Here is something I found yesterday on the subject of PEONies, which I have in my garden. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TJ4YK0iLuDQ

@lucky13

You still don't understand the theme.

@The X-Phile
My thoughts exactly. That's two weeks in a row with double Wednesdays.

@Times Rita

Regarding you having "become...a political junkie since 45 and 47...

A long, long time ago I watched a movie, maybe Dr. Zhivago, about the new Soviet Russia.  In it one of the characters responds to an accusation of "being political" by saying "*everything* is political".  

That phrase "everything is political" just stunned me with dread.  I thought a society that thought of *everything* in political terms sounds like hell.

And it kind of is.

@Times Rita
I see him commenting on various news shows quite often, but when presented with just his last name, like here, I can't quickly come up with his first name.  I've decided that it's because "Jamie" sounds like a child's nickname and lacks the gravitas that his well deserved status in the political hierarchy would seem to warrant.

@Rich in Atlanta Oh! and one more VERY unusual puzzle. A Sunday from August 13, 1989 by Nancy W. Atkinson with the title: "Triple Plays." Can't imagine having any chance at this one. All of the clues for theme answers were.. "Cross Word Puzzle." Some theme answers: COUNTRYRACEPERFECTBOX ONESHEARTPROCESSOROUT EXAMINERFORWORDHEADED REFERENCESOFHONOROVER I grasp some of it but not all of it. Some implied clue/answers combinations: 'CROSS'COUNTRYRACE 'WORD'PERFECT 'PUZZLE'BOX 'CROSS'ONESHEART 'WORD'PROCESSOR 'PUZZLE'OUT Whew. You can try figuring out the rest. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=8/13/1989&g=15&d=D ....

@Katie 

The rest of you go ahead. I'm still in my bathrobe, and I'll have to go down into the basement for my pitchfork.

@Linda Jo
I do agree that the clues were not especially tough...but the clever theme was unique, and it was a fair trade-off for me.
I'm disappointed at the harsh reception...(it's the editors who choose the day) but I think the trick made this Thursday-level.

I last played euchre in...hmm 1968.  So I needed help from MC Raskin to get the "powerful card."

@Linda Jo
We blew past exit 42 yesterday, waved, honked the horn, nothing. Glad we missed the smoke, sorry you’re enduring it again today. The rain was definitely a Godsend

@Jerry

I mostly avoid exit 42 now that Buc-ee's is there.
Absurd traffic, and oddly configured round-abouts.
More pickup trucks than MUSCLE CARS.

Smoke hung around all day, we didn't get rain here. But it changes day to day.

@shaproff

My solve times are getting faster on average too but how do you tell the difference between the puzzles getting easier and you getting better?  You can't play new puzzles from the standpoint of your skill level before you gained experience.

@Chris my approach for “testing” this is to play the archives. I’m pretty good at puzzles and have been for a while, and at my age I don’t think I’m getting better at this point. So when it takes me 50-200% more time to complete a 1994 Thursday vs my average across all puzzles, I conclude that they have gotten easier. YMMV
@shaproff I’m am experienced Thursday solver. This was by no means easy for me. 45 minutes.

@shaproff I tend to agree. When I go back into the archives even 10 years I’m completely stymied, even on Mondays, on a regular basis. I don’t think this is all outdated trivia. But then, if I want a harder puzzle, I know where to go.
@shaproff set my Thursday record today @ 11:03

@Chris If you strike down a person (in a boxing ring, say), you lay them low. Maybe that compound word (lay low) is not in everyone’s vocabulary or local argot.

Breeze and snap both refer to an easy task.
@Chris It’s MASCARPONE anyway.
@Chris It’s mascarpone, not marscapone.
@Chris MASCARPONE would be pretty icky in osso bucco. 😊
@Steve not only did I do the same, but as I did, I was thinking, “Isn’t it time to retire this clue? Aren’t coffee urns the things of 1950s church basement meetings and AA meetings on TV?”

@George Krompacky
I was saved from thinking of an URN by the USSR, already in place....hate to say anything nice about Russia (any iteration) but it did spare me from backtracking.

userLocation: There is such a thing as too much couth.
commentBody:
@George Krompacky
Still alive and well, and dispensing hot brown liquids to weary parents at PTA, eager students at Sunday school, and any place where people want a jolt of caffeine and don't need an almond mocha half-caf with an artistic foam portrait as a vehicle.

@George Krompacky
And Army motor pools. Nothing beats motor SGT coffee/cleaning solvent.

@Steve

Right?  Me too.  It is almost always URN.  I'm glad they switched up for a change, though.

@SBK in TO
Well crafted!
Usually. Not always. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/elk

@Cheyenne
For game animals the 'sportsman's plural' has become conventional. One elk, two elk. One trout, two trout. One quail, two quail.
But nobody says "I saw three sparrow" or "a pair of mole".
I would venture that conventional use of the sportsman's plural does not make it 'wrong' to apply the usual pluralizing rules ('elks'). Just uncommon.
@Charles Peterson I've not previously heard the term "sportsman's plural". Love it! Reminds me of the "royal we".
@Cheyenne Language changes. And you'll drive yourself crazy correcting common usage. That is all.

@Bruce >pejorative<. Not perogative. I can't even blame that on autocorrect.
Actually, that's a pretty good example of a know-it-all face plant.
This is a puzzle; don't expect normal.

@Mean Old Lady, I didn’t see your post yesterday, having not come to the comments. But I, too, did not like that clue. I got it, but I bristled a bit at it. Leis are typically made from plumeria or orchids. I have not seen hibiscus leis before. 

I have all here in my back yard, and have made leis in the past from my two plumeria trees (one pink, one white). They are finally in full bloom again after their winter hibernation, and the pink plumeria flowers look beautiful when they drop and float in the pool!

@Jill
Lucky you!  We are not free from severe frosts despite being in "The South" and if a "false Spring" tempts the shrubs and such into sprouting and blooming, BAM! My hydrangeas really got whacked.  
But about the flowers for a lei:  
My favorites were plumeria because of the wonderful fragrance.  Pikake were nice, and orchids were often seen (but not as sweet-smelling IMHO...When we were living in Hawaii, carnations were the "preferred" LEI for a guy to give his prom date.  No one would ever choose an ungainly flower as large as a hibiscus, which also would be dead within a day.  
Someone commented yesterday about plastic hibiscus leis...if you can imagine...  it was just a dud of a clue.

@ad absurdum---Nice. Speed and The French Connection were my favorites but I would green light all of these.

Now please---twenty five words or less...

All the President's Men
The Exorcist
Capote

Thanks!
A lot of us got MUGged by URN. Right there with you.
21 Across is correct as VCR. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Video_Cassette_Recording
@Dave Munger the word is without "WIN". Winter is a season. So WINLESS SEASON makes sense here.

@Dave Munger
"___TER" is the word WINTER without "WIN". Since Winter is a season, "___TER" is a win-less season.
@Michael Aha! Thanks!
@Seward Parker Great name for a Seattleite. I am obviously clueless about puns so I don't know anything else about it...
@Dave Munger I think it’s referring to a long, dreary time. Winter seems to drag on forever, as a winless season does.
@Dave Munger me too! i solved it but it makes no sense to me.

@Rachel

No.  ___TER is just WINTER minus the WIN.  WINTER is a SEASON, so ___TER is a "win"-less season.

That's all there is to it.  You can't apply your stretchy explanation to the other theme answers.

Also, some people actually like winter, or we'd all be living in Florida.
@Roberto on further reflection, I agree with your take.
@Scott I’ve been doing them since the 70s, so fifty years. I don’t think that I just got better recently. I thought that this was the easiest Thursday puzzle ever.

@Scott

Someone comes up with this suggestion very often, but trust me, when I do archive puzzles and they are much harder even at my current experience level, it’s pretty evident

@Gregory Dunn--I finished it with one of my best Thursday times and never really understood what the constructor was doing with the tricky part. I don't think that's a sign of me getting better at solving.

@Scott

Yeah, I tried that very reasonable suggestion long ago.  

It didn't wash with the top solvers then, doesn't now, and I no longer believe it myself,

I suspect that is a part of the difference between then and now, but only a part, and not the main part.

I've reluctantly agreed, though a lot of archives sampling, that there has indeed been the easing of puzzles.

I don't think it's a dramatic difference.  I actually think that a few clues here and there that are especially troublesome can turn an "easy" puzzle into a "hard" one.  My attempts from previous years are littered with a handful of words here or there, nothing connecting, most of it not right.

It's a matter of not being able to get enough of the puzzle to pave the way for the rest of it.

@Toni
I agree that elk is the most commonly used plural of elk, but I checked several reputable online dictionaries, and they all allow ELKS as well. That's enough for it to be a valid answer.

userLocation: There is such a thing as too much couth.

commentBody: @Asher B. let us freaks have our kinky crosswords where things don't make sense! we only ask for 1 day a week (and sometimes Sundays depending on the constructor)!

@Asher B.

I, for one, having already figured out the theme, immediately got ENDLESS; and the theme also helped  me get STRUGGLE with a few crosses, although I do think that STRUGGLE is a little bit of a stretch to mean endeavor, which certainly doesn’t have to be a struggle.
@Tim Fair.
@Asher B.  I agree with you. Even after finishing the puzzle it didn’t make sense. What’s with the capital letters? Etc? This puzzle’s gag was a failure IMHO.

@Asher B.

I realize that it's not fun having an opinion that not everyone shares.

That said, I hope you'll note that no disparagement or "explanation(s) of why you are wrong", no "accusations of [your] being an enemy of fun" are being offered here.  Only honest attempt to explain why there is some value, to some, in these puzzles.

Controversy is part of the fun of this forum.

@Glenn - Here, I fixed it for you:

A plural of elk is. ELK.

@Glenn and do elk (or elks, for that matter) “bugle?” Is that a thing? I imagine they honk or trumpet, but do people specifically say they “bugle?” It’s never a verb when referring to playing that particular valveless brass instrument.
@Glenn — edit — it turns out elk DO bugle. In fact, they frequently do it in Estes Park.

@Brad
That IS the term for the ELK's "call"==just as a horse whinnies, a beagle bays, and a donkey brays.  (I read a book when I was quite young, entitled _Wapiti, The Elk_...it might have been "More than you want to know about this animal" but oh well.)

@Glenn
The B.P.O.E. may beg to differ with that statement

@Roberto But WINLESS SEASON is the only one that can’t be interpreted as a definition for the clue; ENDEAVOR is a bit weak for being an ENDLESS STRUGGLE, but it would be acceptable. AGELESS BEAUTY is absolutely brilliant, and CARELESS MISTAKE matches the full clue of BAD CAREER MOVE. In fact, these last two simply don’t fit your WINTER justification as I understand it. Perhaps the puzzle masters are referring to the bleakness of December, but to me it looks like a flaw in an otherwise brilliant puzzle.

@Roberto

That's brilliant and makes perfect sense. 
The odd man out would be 
[BAD____ ER MOVE] which would need that BAD be included so that MISTAKE = BAD MOVE.

You're so right – the resulting phrases have meaning on their own, but it has nothing to do with the right answer to the clue which is, in fact, just the end words.

BAD MOVE = MISTAKE
WINTER =SEASON
PAGEANT QUEEN = BEAUTY
ENDEAVOR = STRUGGLE

Or maybe that's not what the constructors intended at all, but it works and I, for one, like it, so thank you for posting your understanding of it!

@sotto voce
BAD____ER MOVE should be BAD CAREER MOVE when the blank is filled in.
@Roberto a WINLESS SEASON can indeed be a WINTER. It is also called a dry spell, a drought, or something else suggesting that nothing is growing, that no fruit appears in the orchard.
@Jerry Herd is too general. It also is a term of venury, such as "a murder of crows."

@JT

It might happen during an Elke summer.

Who knows?

...

@Rich in Atlanta

just finished the xword
popped into comments
for a looksee 
read JT's line...

and the first thing I thought of?
was the thing you wrote!
great minds eh?

OMG did I have a crush on ELKE

and as luck would have it...
my dad did a movie with her
in the 60's

and as luck would have it...
I got to chat with her about 
the coincidence
and photograph her
at a journalism student conference!

she could not have been
sweeter or prettier...

she took my breath away
as playing almost any wind instrument might!
Yes, it is.
https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/elk

Adam,
I'm sure you really meant to write that "elk" is the more commonly used plural, right? That would be true. This is, of course, a puzzle, where one should expect less common words.
@Adam It is how I commonly hear BPOE members refer to themselves/their clubs. But I have never heard it used for the animal.
